era_context: The Hellenistic Age is beginning. Alexander III of Macedon has conquered the Persian Achaemenid Empire and is consolidating control over the eastern Mediterranean, Mesopotamia, Egypt, and the Iranian plateau. The classical Greek city-state system is being superseded by large territorial monarchies. In China, the Warring States period continues with intense interstate competition. In India, the Mauryan Empire is consolidating power under Chandragupta.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones

events.title: Battle of Gaugamela and Fall of Persepolis
events.description: Alexander defeated Darius III at Gaugamela (near modern Mosul) in October 330 BCE, decisively ending Achaemenid military resistance. Darius fled eastward and was later assassinated. Alexander subsequently marched to Persepolis, the ceremonial capital of the Persian Empire, and burned the royal palace—a symbolic act marking the complete conquest of the Persian state. This victory consolidated Alexander's control over the former Persian territories and established him as the undisputed ruler of the eastern Mediterranean and Near East.
events.certainty_note: The battle and burning of Persepolis are attested in multiple near-contemporary Greek sources compiled within 300–400 years of the event. The date of October 330 BCE is well-established in historiographic consensus.
cross_references: Led to 329-001 (Pursuit of Darius and consolidation in Bactria)

events.title: Execution of Darius III and Consolidation of Persian Territories
events.description: Following his flight from Gaugamela, Darius III was pursued eastward by Alexander. In July 330 BCE, Darius was overtaken and killed by Bessus, the satrap of Bactria, who sought to preserve Persian independence. Bessus proclaimed himself successor as Artaxerxes V. Alexander's subsequent pursuit and execution of Bessus (in 329 BCE) eliminated the last organized Persian resistance and secured Alexander's claim to the Persian throne. The death of Darius marked the definitive end of the Achaemenid dynasty after nearly 220 years of rule.
key_figures: Bessus (Satrap of Bactria, later Artaxerxes V)
events.certainty_note: The death of Darius and the succession of Bessus are attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is placed in July 330 BCE by modern scholarship, though ancient sources vary slightly.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya Consolidates Power in Northern India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, with the aid of the political strategist Kautilya (Vishnugupta), consolidated control over the Magadha region and began expanding the Mauryan Empire across northern India. By 330 BCE, Chandragupta had eliminated the Nanda dynasty and was establishing the administrative and military foundations of what would become the largest empire in pre-Islamic India. The Mauryan Empire would eventually extend from the Indus Valley to the Bay of Bengal.
key_figures: Kautilya / Vishnugupta (political strategist and author of Arthashastra)
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Chandragupta's power is attested in later Sanskrit sources and the Puranas. The precise dating of events in the 330s BCE is uncertain; Indian chronology for this period relies on later compilations and is subject to scholarly debate. The Arthashastra may have been compiled centuries after Kautilya's lifetime.

events.title: Qi State Dominance in Warring States China
events.description: During the Warring States period, the state of Qi remained one of the most powerful polities in China. Under the rule of King Xuan of Qi (ruled 319–301 BCE), Qi pursued aggressive military expansion and diplomatic maneuvering. In 330 BCE, Qi was consolidating its position as a major regional power, though it would face increasing pressure from the rising state of Qin to the west. The period was characterized by intensive interstate warfare, diplomatic alliances, and the development of military theory and statecraft.
key_figures: King Xuan of Qi (Qi ruler, r. 319–301 BCE)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are compiled in texts written centuries later. The Shiji is the primary source, but its chronology for this era is subject to scholarly revision. Exact dating of events in 330 BCE is approximate.
cross_references: Contemporary with 330-001 (Alexander's conquests in the west)
Part of broader Warring States competition

disconfirming_evidence: The burning of Persepolis is traditionally attributed to Alexander as a deliberate act of vengeance against Persian aggression in Greece. However, some modern scholars (e.g., based on archaeological evidence and reexamination of sources) suggest the destruction may have been more accidental or less symbolically motivated than later Greek sources imply. The date of Darius's death is given as July 330 BCE in most sources, but some ancient accounts place it slightly differently. The extent of Chandragupta's actual control in 330 BCE is debated; some scholars argue the consolidation of the Mauryan Empire occurred more gradually over the 320s BCE. The traditional dating of Chandragupta's reign relies heavily on later sources and synchronisms with Greek accounts of Alexander, which are themselves uncertain.
historiographic_note: The year 330 BCE is well-documented for the Hellenistic world through Greek and later Greco-Roman sources, though these are not contemporary and reflect Greek perspectives. The burning of Persepolis, in particular, is a heavily mythologized event in Western historiography. For India, sources are sparse and compiled centuries later; Indian chronology for this period is debated among scholars. For China, the Shiji provides a framework, but its accuracy for specific years in the Warring States period is uncertain. The historical record is heavily skewed toward the Mediterranean and Near East; events in sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are largely absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Gaugamela was the culmination of Alexander's campaign against Darius III, which began with the Battle of Issus in 331 BCE
The victory at Gaugamela and burning of Persepolis prompted Alexander's pursuit of Darius and subsequent campaigns in Bactria and Sogdiana
The death of Darius and succession of Bessus led directly to Alexander's pursuit and execution of Bessus in 329 BCE
Chandragupta's consolidation of power in 330 BCE laid the foundation for the rapid expansion of the Mauryan Empire in the following decades
